sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A galvanic cell uses Zn(s)|Zn2+(aq) and Cu2+(aq)|Cu(s) half-cells at 25°C. The standard cell potential is E°cell = 1.10 V and n = 2 electrons. Students vary Q = [Zn2+]/[Cu2+] and use E = E° − (0.0592/n)logQ. They measure a larger cell potential when [Cu2+] is high relative to [Zn2+], corresponding to Q < 1.

explanations.D: Correct. When Q = 1, logQ = 0, so the Nernst correction term is zero. Therefore Ecell = E°cell = 1.10 V.
explanations.A: 1.13 V occurs when Q is less than 1 in this setup.
explanations.B: 1.07 V occurs when Q is greater than 1.
explanations.C: The cell still has its standard potential when Q = 1.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Cell potential for a Zn/Cu galvanic cell at different reaction quotients.

questions.stem: If Q = 0.10, why is the cell potential higher than E°?

choices.A: logQ is negative, making the subtraction term increase Ecell
choices.B: logQ is positive, making Ecell larger

explanations.A: Correct. For Q = 0.10, logQ = −1. The term −(0.0592/2)logQ becomes positive, so Ecell is greater than E°. This corresponds to product concentrations being relatively low for the written cell reaction.
explanations.C: n remains 2 electrons for this redox reaction.
explanations.D: Q < 1 often means the spontaneous forward reaction is favored, not stopped.

questions.stem: Which species is reduced in the Zn/Cu galvanic cell?

explanations.C: Correct. In the standard Zn/Cu galvanic cell, Zn is oxidized to Zn2+ and Cu2+ is reduced to Cu(s). Reduction is gain of electrons, so Cu2+ is the species reduced.
explanations.A: Zn(s) loses electrons, so it is oxidized rather than reduced.
explanations.B: Zn2+ is the product of oxidation at the anode.
explanations.D: Cu(s) is the reduced product, not the species gaining electrons.

questions.stem: What happens to Ecell as Q increases from 0.10 to 10?

choices.D: It becomes exactly equal to zero

explanations.B: Correct. The Nernst equation shows that increasing Q makes the correction term more negative for this spontaneous cell. The graph drops from about 1.13 V at Q = 0.10 to about 1.07 V at Q = 10.
explanations.A: The graph shows a decrease, not an increase.
explanations.C: Ecell changes with Q under nonstandard conditions.
explanations.D: The shown values remain near 1.1 V and do not reach zero.

questions.stem: At equilibrium, what is true of the galvanic cell potential?

choices.A: Ecell = 0 because no net driving force remains
choices.D: Electrons stop existing in the cell

explanations.A: Correct. At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and ΔG = −nFE, so Ecell = 0. The reaction quotient has reached the equilibrium constant, eliminating net electrochemical driving force.
explanations.B: Ecell equals E° only under standard conditions with Q = 1, not at equilibrium in general.
explanations.C: Q is not zero at equilibrium; it equals Keq.
explanations.D: Electrons still exist, but there is no net spontaneous cell reaction.

questions.passage: In an electrolysis experiment, Cu2+(aq) is reduced to Cu(s) at an electrode according to Cu2+ + 2e− → Cu(s). A current of 1.93 A is passed for 1000 s. Students use Faraday’s constant, 96,500 C/mol e−, and copper’s molar mass, 63.5 g/mol, to calculate the mass deposited. They assume nearly 100% current efficiency.
questions.stem: How many coulombs of charge pass during the 1000 s electrolysis?

explanations.D: Correct. Charge is Q = It = 1.93 A × 1000 s = 1930 C. One ampere is one coulomb per second.
explanations.B: 96,500 C is Faraday’s constant per mole of electrons, not the charge passed.
explanations.C: 193,000 C is too large by a factor of 100.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Predicted mass of copper deposited by a 1.93 A current over time.

questions.stem: How many moles of electrons pass through the circuit?

explanations.C: Correct. Moles of electrons equal charge divided by Faraday’s constant: 1930/96,500 ≈ 0.020 mol e−.
explanations.A: 0.010 mol is the amount of copper deposited, not moles of electrons.
explanations.B: 0.040 mol doubles the correct electron amount.
explanations.D: 2.0 mol is too large by two orders of magnitude.

questions.stem: What mass of copper is deposited?

explanations.B: Correct. Cu2+ requires 2 mol e− per mole Cu. Thus 0.020 mol e− deposits 0.010 mol Cu, with mass 0.010 × 63.5 = 0.635 g.
explanations.A: 1.27 g would ignore the two-electron requirement and treat 0.020 mol e− as 0.020 mol Cu.
explanations.C: 0.020 g confuses moles of electrons with grams of copper.
explanations.D: 63.5 g would require 1 mole of Cu, far more charge than supplied.

questions.stem: What is occurring at the electrode where copper plates out?

explanations.D: Correct. Copper ions gain electrons to form solid copper, so the plating electrode is where reduction occurs. The half-reaction explicitly shows electrons as reactants.
explanations.A: Oxidation is loss of electrons; the copper ion is gaining electrons.
explanations.B: Neutralization is an acid-base process, not the redox process here.
explanations.C: Copper deposition here requires electron transfer and is not simply precipitation.

questions.stem: If current efficiency were only 80%, how would the actual mass deposited compare with the calculated mass?

choices.A: It would be 80% of the calculated mass
choices.B: It would be 125% of the calculated mass

explanations.A: Correct. Current efficiency describes the fraction of charge that produces the desired reaction. At 80% efficiency, only 80% of the theoretical copper mass would be deposited.
explanations.B: 125% would be the reciprocal and would overstate deposition.
explanations.C: Lower efficiency means some current goes to other processes, so mass would change.
explanations.D: Mass deposited cannot be negative; lower efficiency simply reduces it.
